summary: A breakdown of Aristotle's ethical philosophy, focusing on the pursuit of 'living well' or happiness as the ultimate end goal of human life. It explores his distinction between real and apparent goods, the necessity of a life plan, and the cultivation of virtues like temperance, courage, and justice through habitual action.

principles.statement: The ultimate end goal of life is living well (happiness).
principles.rationale: Everything we do is either an end or a means to an end. We desire happiness solely for its own sake, not as a requisite for achieving some further goal.
principles.source_quote: We deliberate not about ends but about means. For a doctor does not deliberate whether he shall heal... They assume the end and consider how and by what means it is to be attained

principles.statement: An unplanned life is not worth examining.
principles.rationale: To achieve mastery in living, we must have a definitive plan that outlines our priorities, distinguishes our goals, and figures out how to achieve them.

principles.statement: Real goods fulfill natural desires, while apparent goods fulfill acquired desires.
principles.rationale: Natural desires (needs like food and shelter) are universally good for all humans regardless of background. Acquired desires (wants) are based on individual opinion and only appear to be good at the time.
principles.source_quote: For the apparent good is the object of appetite, and the real good is the primary object of rational wish. But desire is consequent on opinion rather than opinion on desire; for the thinking is the starting point.

frameworks.name: The Plan for Living Well
frameworks.when_to_apply: When structuring your life to achieve true happiness.
steps: Identify the ultimate end goal, which is living well or happiness.
Acquire 'external goods' (food, drink, clothing, sleep, shelter) to maintain 'bodily goods' (vigor, vitality, health).
Acquire 'goods of the soul' (knowledge, friendship, legitimate self-esteem/honor) to nurture your unique human ability to think and reason.
Develop virtuous habits (temperance, courage, justice) to correctly manage and balance these goods.
Acknowledge that luck plays a role in your circumstances, but use virtuous behavior to maximize favorable outcomes.

mental_models.name: Means vs. Ends
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating human actions by distinguishing between things done for the sake of getting something else (means) and things done solely for their own sake (ends).
mental_models.example: A doctor does not deliberate about whether he should heal (the end), but rather considers by what means healing is most easily and best produced.

mental_models.name: Real vs. Apparent Goods
mental_models.description: A distinction between what humans actually need to survive and flourish (real goods/natural desires) versus what they merely think will enrich their lives based on subjective experience (apparent goods/acquired desires).
mental_models.example: A double bacon cheeseburger might seem like a good idea at the time (apparent good), but it is not actually good for long-term health compared to basic sustenance (real good).

heuristics: Make a habit of acting virtuously so that it becomes second nature.
Deliberate about the means to achieve your goals, not the end goals themselves.

signature_quotes.text: We deliberate not about ends but about means. For a doctor does not deliberate whether he shall heal, nor an orator whether he shall persuade, nor a statesman whether he shall produce law and order, nor does anyone else deliberate about his end.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how humans navigate the means to ultimately get to the end goal of life.

signature_quotes.text: The primary objects of desire and of thought are the same. For the apparent good is the object of appetite, and the real good is the primary object of rational wish.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the difference between real goods (needs) and apparent goods (wants).

signature_quotes.text: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the idea that virtue and living a good life require consistent, habitual action.

anti_patterns.description: Seeking honor or fame without a legitimate reason or substance.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Honor is a 'good of the soul' only when it is gained for a legitimate reason, such as skill or virtue. Seeking fame for doing absolutely nothing is a vice, not a real good.

anti_patterns.description: Living life without a definitive plan or end goal.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Without knowing what you are trying to do, why, or how you are going to get there, you cannot achieve the highest level of happiness or mastery in living.

anti_patterns.description: Doing things in excess rather than practicing temperance.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Most human problems, whether health-wise or in relationships, occur when we do things in excess. Failing to exercise moderation prevents us from living well.
